 PREFACE
The Member airlines of the International Air Transport Association (IATA) and the Airlines for America (A4A) have developed the Standard IATA/A4A Cargo Interchange Message Procedures (Cargo-IMP).
The purpose of this Manual is to enable communication which will ensure uniformity, understanding, accuracy and economy. The Manual is published as Attachment `A' to IATA Resolution 670.
 The 34th Edition of the IATA Cargo Interchange Message Procedures Manual
becomes effective on 1 January 2015 and replaces the previous 33rd Edition.
 As per the IATA Cargo Services Conference (CSC), there will be no further
changes to Cargo-IMP Messages and the Cargo-IMP 34th edition is the last and final edition. From now on, any future changes in the messages will only be conducted in the Cargo-XML standards that are available in the IATA Cargo-XML Manual and Toolkit.
 Users are advised that the Cargo-IMP functionality is improved by allowing
exchange of ASCII 7 bit characters.
 Users are further advised that additional editorial corrections have been made to
the Status Answer (FSA) and the Status Update (FSU) messages.
These procedures are consistent with the requirements of the IATA Systems and Communications Reference Manuals as they relate to technical communications procedures, and with IATA and A4A Cargo Resolutions, as they relate to cargo processes.
Source Documents take precedence over codes published in this Manual.
Messages in the Cargo-IMP format will be accepted by both IATA Members and A4A Members.
Proposals for new messages or amendments to existing messages published in the Cargo-IMP Manual shall be submitted to the Cargo Business Processes Panel (CBPP), on the standard form which is Attachment `A' of Resolution 671 and is included with this Preface.
The grey shaded background indicates amendments that have not yet been declared effective at time of production. They are awaiting the necessary government approvals.
Any comments concerning the content of the Manual would be appreciated. Users of the Manual are invited to contact (YMQFPXB or cargoedi@iata.org) for any technical assistance required in the implementation and use of these messages.

INSTRUCTIONS FOR COMPLETION
Data ID -- organise data by logical, related groups and enter either:
an upper case letter to identify the data group; or a sequential number for each data element.
Data Description -- enter a concise, meaningful description of the data group or the data element.
Form -- enter the character type (a ­ alpha, n ­ numeric, an ­ alphanumeric, d ­ decimal, t ­ text) for the data element and the number, or minimum/maximum range, e.g. 4...8, of characters.
Status -- enter one of the following statuses for the data group or data element:
-- mandatory (must be included); -- conditional (must be included if the indicated condition is met); -- optional (may be included).
The status of the data element is relevant to the status of the data group. For example, within an optional data group, the condition for a conditional data element only applies if the optional data group is included.
For EDIFACT messages, only mandatory and conditional statuses apply.
Condition -- if the status of the data group or element is "C", enter the condition for the inclusion of the data group or data element. The condition must be related strictly to the existence, or non-existence, of other data within the message.
Note -- if further explanation for a data group or data element is required, enter a sequential reference number and include the explanatory note on a separate sheet of paper.
Repeat Information -- if multiple occurrences of data are allowed, complete this as follows:

Data -- enter the Data ID, or range of Data IDs, of the data group(s) or data element(s) that can be repeated;
Times -- enter the number of times the data can be repeated. If unlimited occurrences are allowed, enter "U";
Within -- for data repetition within a data group or within another data repetition range, enter the Data ID, or range of Data IDs, of the data group(s).

GENERAL INFORMATION
1. FUNCTION OF CARGO-IMP MESSAGES
The Cargo-IMP messages have been developed by the Member Airlines of the International Air Transport Association (IATA) and the Airlines for America (A4A) as Standard IATA/A4A Cargo Interchange Message Procedures. The purpose of these messages is to ensure uniformity, mutual understanding, accuracy and economy in inter-airline data exchange and in data exchange between airlines and other air cargo industry participants including agents, brokers and Customs. The messages are used in both manual and computerized environments.
The messages are consistent with the A4A/IATA Cargo Resolutions as they relate to Cargo Procedures.
 Note: IATA has now developed and published the Cargo-XML Messages. For
further details visit IATA Cargo-XML webpage at www.iata.org/Cargo-XML.
2. TERMINOLOGY
For the purposes of this Manual the following terminology will be used:
2.1 Transmission
A transmission consists of the data (information) that is interchanged at one time between parties.
2.2 Message
A message is the lowest unit of complete information that may be exchanged between an originator and a recipient for a predefined purpose.
2.3 Standard Message Identifier (SMI)
An SMI is an approved three-letter code which is used to uniquely identify a given type of message. It must always be shown at the beginning of a Standard Message Text and appears separately on the first line after the address envelope.
2.4 Standard Message Text (SMT)
A Standard Message Text (SMT) contains the information required for a predefined purpose. It is composed of a number of related text elements structured according to agreed standards. The sequence in which elements are grouped is determined by the logical progression required for computer processing as well as manual reading.
2.5 Text Element
A text element is a basic unit for the transmission of data and is composed of a group of related data elements.

2.6 Text Element Identifier (Line Identifier)
A three-letter identifier used at the beginning of a line to indicate that certain data elements will follow.

2.7 Data Group
A data group consists of either one or several data elements with or without separators. It shows the interdependence of data within a text element and allows the group to be shown as optional, conditional or mandatory.

2.8 Data Element (Simple and Composite)

A data element is an item of information that has been uniquely identified for the interchange of data. A data element may be either a Simple or a Composite Data Element.

Simple Data Element -- One unit of data, providing a single concept, e.g. Month MAR.

Composite Data Element

-- A combination of more than one unit of data, providing a broad concept, e.g. Full Date DDMMMYY 12MAR85.

2.9 Data Element Identifier
A one, two, three or four-letter identifier used to identify the Data Element which follows.

2.10 Optional
The Text Element/Data Group/Data Element may be included at the option of the sender or by agreement between sender and recipient.

2.11 Conditional
The Text Element/Data Group/Data Element is required dependent upon a stated condition.

2.12 Mandatory
The Text Element/Data Group/Data Element must be included.

2.13 Separator Character
A special character which serves as a separator between Data Elements/Data Groups/Text Elements.

2.14 Syntax Rules
The agreed rules governing the structure of a transmission.

3. TRANSMISSION OF CARGO-IMP MESSAGES
3.1 Various Applications for Message Utilisation
A Cargo-IMP Message may be utilised in the following ways:
3.1.1 Computer-to-Computer The Cargo-IMP Message is automatically constructed and generated by the sending party and will be automatically processed by the receiving party.
3.1.2 Manual-to-Computer
The Cargo-IMP Message is manually constructed and generated by the sending party and will be automatically processed by the receiving party.
3.1.3 Computer-to-Manual
The Cargo-IMP Message is automatically constructed and generated by the sending party and will be manually processed by the receiving party.
3.1.4 Manual-to-Manual
The Cargo-IMP Message is manually constructed and generated by the sending party and will be manually processed by the receiving party.
4. DESCRIPTION OF CARGO-IMP MESSAGES -- SYNTAX RULES
4.1 Composition of Cargo-IMP Messages
A Cargo-IMP Message is composed of a Message Address, a Standard Message Identifier (SMI) and a Standard Message Text (SMT). The Standard Message Identifier is shown separately on the first line of the Cargo-IMP Message.
The structure of Cargo-IMP Messages is such that they can be easily prepared and read in both manual and computerized environments.
Cargo-IMP Messages are constructed with due regard to transmission costs and therefore the number of characters is kept to a minimum.
4.2 Description of Cargo-IMP Messages
The message descriptions/specifications contained in the message chapters of this manual detail the SMI and the sequential order of data elements that form a Standard Message Text (SMT). The sequential order is determined by the logical progression of elements required for computer processing and also the requirements of manual reading.
 4.3 Character Set
Cargo-IMP Messages published in Cargo-IMP Manual 32nd edition and above (including FWB version 16) can use ASCII 7 bit (Table 4.3.1) characters while

messages published in lower editions of the Cargo-IMP Manual can continue using the limited characters.

Cargo-IMP Messages published in Cargo-IMP Manual 31st edtion and lower (except FWB version 16) can only contain the following approved characters:

the letters A to Z (upper case only) the numerals 0 to 9 the special characters / -. Space < =

4.4 Separator Characters

Some of the approved characters are used for specific functions within the message.

The slant (/) is used as an element separator. The slant is also used as the first character position on a line to indicate that the data which follows is a continuation of related data in the preceding line, i.e. the data has only been entered on a separate line because of the line length constraints.

The hyphen (-) is used as a sub-element separator.

The full stop (.) is used as a decimal point within data elements.
The carriage return line feed (< =) is used to separate lines of information. Where possible, a "line" contains a complete logical group of information.

 4.5 Element Character Format

Standardised symbols are used in the message description to describe character formats. The symbols and their meaning are detailed below:

a

represents a single alphabetic character;

n

represents a single numeric character;

m t

represents only mixed alpha (characters A through Z) and figures (numerals 0 through 9);
represents a character in free form text (alphabetic, numeric, hyphen, full stop or space) for Cargo-IMP Manual 31st edition and lower (excluding FWB version 16); represents ASCII characters (see 4.3.1) for Cargo-IMP Manual 32nd edition and above (including FWB version 16);

< indicates a Carriage Return;

= indicates a Line Feed onto the next line.

The following indicators are used in conjunction with the above symbols:

[ ] brackets framing symbols indicate the optional status of the characters, e.g. [aa] represents 2 optional alphabetic characters;

[N] [...N]

indicates a number N of characters or group of characters; e.g. a[N] represents a number N of alphabetic characters, aaa[N] represents a number N of the group of 3 alphabetic character;
indicates a number of characters or group of characters up to and including a number N;

[M...N] indicates a number of characters or group of characters where M is the lower limit and N is the upper limit inclusively;

n

indicates a 12 numeric element which could include a decimal

[...12] point.

p

4.6 Identification within a Cargo-IMP Message
Data within a Cargo-IMP message is identified in one of two ways:
(a) Implicitly -- by position in a fixed sequence; (b) Explicitly -- by use of a Data Element or Text Element Identifier (Line
Identifier).
Data Element Identifiers consist of one or more characters.
Text Element Identifiers (Line Identifiers) consist of three characters and identify a single line or the first line in a sequence of lines. These identifiers are always followed by a separator (/).

4.7 Specification of Content of a Cargo-IMP Message
For the purpose of this manual the following symbols are used to define the status of the Element:
O -- Optional (see 2.10) C -- Conditional (see 2.11) M -- Mandatory (see 2.12).
The elements in the message are grouped by levels where:
Level 1 is the Text Element Level Level 2 is the Data Group Level Level 3 is the Data Element Level.
Each element is allocated a numeric reference within the Cargo-IMP Message. For example, a ULD Description might be:

4. 4.1 4.2 4.2.1 4.2.2 4.2.3 4.3 4.4 4.4.1 4.4.2

ULD Description Total Number of ULDs ULD Identification
ULD Type ULD Serial Number ULD Owner Code ULD Position Restriction Indicator Weight of ULD Weight Code Weight

Level 1 Level 2 Level 2 Level 3 Level 3 Level 3 Level 2 Level 2 Level 3 Level 3

4.8 Length of a Cargo-IMP Message
A Cargo-IMP Message is restricted to a maximum of 3500 characters. Each line within a Cargo-IMP Message is restricted to a maximum of 69 characters.

4.9 Split of a Cargo-IMP Message
If a message is longer than 3500 characters, the method used to split the message text is dependent on the Message Identifier.
4.9.1 Method 1
For Airline Flight Manifest (FFM), Booking List (FBL) and Declaration for Dangerous Goods Data (FDD) Messages the following method is used:
First Part:
(a) the first line of the Standard Message Text (SMT) must contain sequence number: 1
(b) the Standard Message Text (SMT) must terminate with the line: CONT < =
Following Parts:
(a) the first line of the Standard Message Text (SMT) must contain the next sequential number: N
(b) the Standard Message Text (SMT) must terminate with the line:
either CONT < = if other part to follow or LAST < = if the last part

Note: If the message has only 1 part, the SMT terminates with the line: LAST < =
4.9.2 Method 2 For all other messages, the following method is used:

First Part: must terminate with the extra line: PART/1/CONT < = Following Parts: (a) must commence with the SMI plus the first line of the Standard Message Text
(SMT) (b) must terminate with the extra line:
· either PART/N/CONT < = · if other part to follow · or PART/N/LAST < = · if the last part
4.10 Illustration of Standard Form Structure
5. TEXT ELEMENTS FOR FREE TEXT DATA
There are two special text elements which occur in several Cargo-IMP Messages. These text elements commence with a text element identifier (SSR or OSI) followed by a separator (/) and terminate with a CRLF (< =). These text elements are: (a) SSR -- Special Service Request (special manual action required message will
have to be routed for manual processing);

(b) OSI -- Other Service Information (can simply be stored as remarks -- message does not have to be routed for any special action).
6. VALIDATION OF A CARGO-IMP MESSAGE
6.1 Validation of Data to be Built into a Cargo-IMP Message
In a computer environment validation checks according to the Resolutions and/or Recommended Practices published in the IATA Cargo Services Conference Resolutions Manual should be applied prior to building data into a Cargo-IMP message.
6.2 Validation of the Structure of a Cargo-IMP Message
Before a message is transmitted, certain validation routines are performed on the message in order to minimize the possible errors in message composition. The validation routines include:
(a) verification of the length of the message; (b) verification of the length of each line in the message; (c) verification of the characters used in the message.
6.2.1 Validation of Repeated Data
Within the message specifications there is sometimes an allowance for data to be repeated. Unless the number of occurrences is specified, e.g. Can occur a maximum of three times, the number of repeats is unlimited but must adhere to the following:
 (a) the maximum 69 characters per line and 3500 characters per message as
specified in 4.8; (b) a specific note limiting the total number of characters within the data group or
reference line.
7. RETRANSMITTAL OF A MESSAGE
When a receiving station requests that a message be retransmitted, the retransmitted message text may start with the code "PDM" followed by a Carriage Return Line Feed.
When a sender or sending system retransmits a message because of a lack of confirmation of receipt, the retransmitted message text must start with the code "PDM" followed by a Carriage Return Line Feed.
8. CORRECTION OF INVALID/INCORRECT REFERENCE NUMBER
When the receiving system cannot action the incoming message due to an invalid or incorrect prime reference number, i.e. AWB Identification, Embargo Number or ULD Reference Number, it is recommended that the standard Cargo-IMP Error (FNA) Message is transmitted to the sender of the message.

10. TRANSMISSION OF CARGO-IMP MESSAGES THROUGH AIRLINE DATA EXCHANGE NETWORKS

When a Cargo-IMP Message is transmitted through the airline data exchange networks it is "enveloped" between a "Message Address" header (comprising a message priority indicator, one or more address groups, an origin group and a date/time group) and an "End of Message" trailer.
The definition of the airline data exchange networks envelope is contained in the IATA Systems and Communications Reference Manual. The following descriptions are extracted from, or are in line with, the information contained in that Manual.
10.1 Message Address (Header) Format

10.2 Message Priority Indicator

The following "Priority Indicators" may be included in a position prior to the first address in the address section:

Indicator QU

Precedence URGENT

QP

NORMAL

Note: The nature of the traffic bearing this priority indicator is such that the originator deems that it should be given special attention in local delivery, and that this delivery should not be delayed.

QK

NORMAL

Note: Default for `normal' message traffic. QK has same precedence as messages bearing no priority indicator.

QD

DEFERRED

The Priority Indicators QX and QS have distinct meanings in some systems; QX is used for urgent operational traffic and QS for top precedence messages pertaining to the safety of human life.

10.3 Address and Origin Groups
The Address and Origin Groups consist of seven-character groups (these may be extended to eight-letter groups in the future) of which:
(a) the first three letters shall be a Location Identifier; (b) the fourth and fifth characters denote the Office Function Designator
(department) within the airline; (c) the sixth and seventh characters (or sixth, seventh and eighth letters when
used) denote the airline, or appropriate organization.

Note: Where automated carriers require particular Cargo-IMP Messages to be sent to a specific SITA/ARINC address, address details are included in the appropriate message description contained in the individual message chapter.
10.4 Message Identity
The message identity is used by the originator to identify the message. There is no standard format although the date/time is often used. The message identity can include any alphanumeric characters plus the full stop (with the exception of upper case H and upper case D).
The use of the oblique (/) character in the message identity is not recommended. If there is no double signature, the message identity must not include an oblique (/) character as the third character following 2 letters as this is read as a double signature.
If the date/time group is used in the message identity, the time must be expressed in UTC.
11. TRANSMISSION OF MESSAGES IN A CCS ENVIRONMENT
To facilitate the role of CCSs in converting between different message standards, i.e. Cargo-IMP and EDIFACT, all messages routed through CCSs are "wrapped" in a standardized EDIFACT envelope as shown below.
11.1 Use of the EDIFACT Envelope
The EDIFACT UNB (interchange header) and UNZ (interchange trailer) segments

are used to envelope EDIFACT Message Interchanges, as shown in the following Interchange example:

For air cargo applications the message sender and recipient are identified in the UNB segment through the PIMA (Participant Identification and Message Addressing) scheme, as shown below.

The 35 character fields for data elements 0004 (Sender Identification) and 0010 (Recipient Identification) are structured, in accordance with the standard `Participant Identification and Message Addressing (PIMA)' Scheme. The PIMA Scheme is limited to 33 characters, and is structured as follows:

11.6 Examples of UN/EDIFACT Enveloping
(a) Request from an agent to an airline: UNB+UNOA:1+CCHAGT03DANZASBERNE/BRN08:ZI+CCHAIRO1ZRHFMSR:Z1+92062 1' UNH+FFR001+IFTMBF:2:912:UN:IATA01' 27SEGMENTS' UNT+29+FFROO1' UNZ+1+AGTOO1'
(b) Answer from the airline to the agent: UNB+UNOA:1+CCHAIRO1ZRHFMSR:Z1+CCHAGT03DANZASBERNE/BRN08:Z1+92062

1' UNH+FFAOO1+IFTMBC:2:912:UN:IATA01' 18SEGMENTS' UNT+20+FFA001' UNZ+1+AIR001'
11.7 Cargo-IMP Messages Transmitted Under EDIFACT Envelope
Cargo-IMP messages routed through CCSs are wrapped within the EDIFACT envelope as explained below.
11.8 Wrapping of Cargo-IMP Messages
Each fraction of 5 lines of a Cargo-IMP Message will be included in a FTX segment with the resulting FTX segment(s) preceded by a UNH segment and terminated by a UNT segment.
When packing these fractions of 5 Cargo-IMP lines into one FTX segment the various CRLFs shall be replaced by " : " except the last one which will be replaced by " ' ".
11.9 Example of Wrapping of Cargo-IMP Message
(a) Cargo-IMP message before wrapping: FFA/2 021-7777777OMSPLHR/P5K575OT9 AA001/19MAR/JFKLHR/KK SSR/TEMP RESTRICTION OK /SPL CARE OK OSI/NEED TO RECEIVE CGO BY 1800 19 MAR /AND TO MEET THE AGENT REF/MSPFCNW/4923ACA
(b) Cargo-IMP message after wrapping: UNH+FFA911+CIMFFA:O:O:IA' FTX+CIM+++FFA/2:021-7777777OMSPLHR/P5K575OT9:AA001/19MAR /JFKLHR/KK:SSR/TEMP RESTRICTION OK:/SPL CARE OK' FTX+CIM+++OSI/NEED TO RECEIVE CGO BY 1800 19 MAR; /AND TO MEET THE AGENT:REF/MSPFCNW/4923ACA' UNT+4+FFA911'
11.10 Use of the EDIFACT Message Header
The EDIFACT UNH message header is used to start, identify and specify a message.

-- any other 6-character identifier in cases where there is no Cargo-IMP equivalent

12. RETURN INFORMATION FIELD (`RIF') ADDRESSING STANDARD FOR CCS/PARTICIPANT COMMUNICATION
For messages which are converted between EDIFACT and Cargo-IMP standards, and then routed to/ from CCS `participant' systems, the `RIF' Standard is applied in order to retain `PIMA' address information within the message envelopes. CCS `participant' is defined within this context as an airline or user of a SITA/ARINC Type `B' connection.
The `RIF' Standard provides the transfer, upon message conversion, of `PIMA' data between the EDIFACT envelope and the Type `B' IATA envelope. This transfer is necessary in order to retain sender and addressee address information throughout the message transmission cycle, from sender to destination and back via the response message, and several CCSs as necessary.
The following benefits accrue to airline CCS users, through the use of the `RIF' Standard:

-- provides onward routing of messages to parties who do not have their own SITA/ARINC address, but who support EDIFACT envelopes; and
-- allows unique identification of EDIFACT end-users for purposes of security checking (ex. to check if a party requesting a booking is authorized to do so);
Similarly the following benefits accrue to agents:
-- enables a requesting agent to forward the message response to the originating customers' terminal/file/representative.
12.1 Description of the `RIF' Standard -- EDIFACT Envelope
The following is return address information which is structured within the EDIFACT UNB Segment:

UN/EDIFACT Data element
S002 INTERCHANGE SENDER - or S003 INTERCHANGE RECIPIENT 0004 Sender identification(*) - or0010 Recipient identification(*) 0007 Partner ID code qualifier 0008 Address for Reverse Routing(**) - or 0014 Routing Address(**)

M/C

Format Length

Used

M

M

M

an..35 33

M

an..35 33

C

an.. 4 4

C

an..14 14

C

an..14 14

(*): Data elements 0004/0010 represent the `PIMA' addressing scheme. (**): Data elements 0008 and 0014 are referred to generically as the `Auxiliary Routing Address'. Data element 0008 is used for incoming messages while 0014 is used for responses.
Example -- EDIFACT Envelope (from UNB segment):
CGNTRK09TULIPTRUCKERCOMPANY/AMS99:PIMA:WIMSMAINOFFICE

12.2 `RIF' Standard -- IATA Envelope

The RIF Standard provides for placement of the EDIFACT envelope address information (less data element 0007) into the `Message Identity' portion of the IATA envelope. In transfering this data, the `slash' separating the CCS Participant Identifier and Airport/City Code within the PIMA Scheme, is replaced by a `period'. This ensures that the `slash' separator is uniquely used, within the Type `B' envelope, to indicate the end of the Double Signature Group. The resulting `Origin Line' of the IATA envelope is structured as follows:
13. PRESENTATION OF DIRECTORY 13.1 Presentation of Data Elements in the Directory
Each identified data element has been given: (a) a data element name; (b) a description of the concept to explain the agreed meaning and to aid in
determining the content of the information (data item) to be provided with the data element;

(c) a specification of the character representation of the data item, with indication of space (in number of characters) available and, when applicable or required:
(d) references which refer users to other parts of the Directory, or to other sources, as appropriate;
(e) synonyms of data element names where such are widely used.
It is assumed that most data elements can be expressed either in plain language or in code, and it is recognized that there will be a need to refer to either of these versions, and sometimes to both.
Mainly for identification and management purposes, each data element is provided with a numeric identifier (tag).

13.2 Layout of Data Element Entries

The first line of each data element entry contains the numeric tag and the second line contains the name of the data element.

In addition, each data element entry contains a number of parts, identified by a header. The following headers are used to designate the different parts of the entries:

"Alt. Name"

= synonymous names of the data element;

"Description"

= description of the data element;

"Format"

= character representation, space available;

"Cargo-IMP Ref."

= reference to an indicated source of information -- usually the Coding section;

"Note"

= additional information, when appropriate.

Data element entries are separated from each other by a line.

13.3 Abbreviations of Character Representations
See Element Character Format of General Information for more details concerning the abbreviations used to explain character representation.

14. CATEGORIES OF DATA ELEMENTS

14.1 Categories of Data Elements, Allocation of Identifiers (Tags)
Three-digit numbers are used as data element identifiers (tags) in the Directory. Taking into account the desirability of grouping the data elements into broad categories in a way that will assist users of the Directory, the numbers have been assigned as follows:
(a) 000 to 099 are not used; (b) 100 to 999 are used for random allocation of tags to the data elements, within
the framework of a sub-division into 9 general categories, as specified in 14.3.
The purpose of the tags is to provide language independent identifiers for the data

elements; the only significance attributed to the numbers is their designation of broad categories.
14.2 Grouping of Data Elements into Broad Categories
The system selected for the Directory for grouping data elements into broad categories is based on the characteristics of the data item, taking into account its nature rather than its application area. This means that all dates are placed in one group, all names in another, and all amounts in a third, without any consideration of the kind of date, name and amount.
Users, even if they do not know the exact name of a data element, or in which application area a particular data element might have been placed, are likely to know the expected contents of the data item with sufficient accuracy to be able to find it within a limited range of not more than one hundred data elements placed together according to this principle.
14.3 Category Description
As set out in 14.2 the data elements have been grouped into broad categories in a way that will assist users of the Directory, at the same time taking into account the need to reserve substantial capacity for future newly defined data elements.
The following grouping is used in the Directory:
Group 1: (100­149) Documentation, references
Data elements intended for data handling or documentation purposes, such as document and message names, references and reference numbers, ordinal numbers such as item and page numbers, number of copies of documents.
Group 2: (200­249) Dates, times, periods of time
All expressions of time, such as calendar dates, periods of time (month, week, day), time limits, commencement and expiry dates.
Group 3: (300­349) Parties, addresses, places, countries
Names and addresses of legal and physical persons, designations of officials, organizational units, places, countries, routes. Group 4: (400­449) Clauses, conditions, terms, instructions
Authentications, authorizations, endorsements, certifications, clauses, conditions, terms, reference clauses, stamps, labels, requests, instructions, receipts, statements, information texts.
Group 5: (500­549) Amounts, charges, percentages
Financial values, amounts, charges; other quantities and values for commercial, transport, customs, statistical and

any other purposes; other details such as prices, rates, percentages and discounts, used as a basis for invoice calculations, etc.
Group 6: (600­649) Measure identifiers, quantities (other than monetary)
Dimensions, weights, volumes, distances, temperature, currency, other quantities, with the exception of monetary quantities in Group 5, measure unit specifiers. Group 7: (700­749) Goods and articles: descriptions and identifiers
Descriptions, classifications and identification of goods and articles, consignment identifiers, number and kind of packages, dangerous goods details.
Group 8: (800­849) Transport modes and means, ULDs (aircraft containers and pallets)
Identification and description of means of transport and ULDs, transport equipment details, transport modes and movements, voyage and flight numbers.
Group 9: (900­949) External Requirements (Customs, etc.)
Data elements used for the interchange of information with external parties such as Customs Authorities, Agents, etc.
14.4 Sub-Data Elements
The Sub-Data Elements represent the description used to identify the variations of the Data Elements used in the "Element" column of the Message Specifications.
15. MESSAGE TYPE VERSION NUMBERS
The Message Type Version Number was first introduced in the 9th Edition of the Cargo-IMP Manual. All new Message Specifications in that Edition, and those that had been amended from the 8th Edition, included a Message Type Version Number.
All other messages do not include the version number .
16. EXCHANGE PARTIES CAPABILITIES
Message Exchange Parties can be airlines or third parties (such as handling agents) with computer systems that are capable of interchanging Cargo-IMP Messages.
The individual user tables indicate the information available at the time of publication of this Edition of the CIMP. Any additional information may be obtained from the author, whose address is included in the Preface of this product.
As the Cargo-IMP users included may not be exhaustive, parties are asked to send identification of further Cargo-IMP users to the author as well.

The user tables contain the message version numbers for Cargo-IMP messages supported by users of the Cargo-IMP standard.
Cargo-IMP users are presented as two separate groups:
· Airlines · Forwarders, Handling Agents and Third Parties
Two addresses are indicated:
· the address for the Cargo Control Computer · the address for the Cargo Data Interchange Coordinator for each system
For parties wishing to exchange messages with airlines, it is recommended that communication first be made with the Airline Respondant/Coordinator indicated.
The following is a list of symbols used in this section. They indicate the status of support for a given message version, for each particular party. T = supported for transmission only R = supported for receiving and processing only B = supported for both Transmission and Receiving x = does not exist, and is therefore, "not-applicable" ­ = not supported
An asterisk (*) in the user's table indicates that a special condition applies to that particular message or exchange party. The details are to be found following the table.
17. "GENERAL" NOTES
Introduction to "General" Notes
The specification for each Cargo-IMP message includes a "Note" column. Most notes referenced in the column are specific to one message, e.g. FWB1 or FFM1. "Message Specific" notes and "Conditional and Qualification" notes are found at the end of each message specification.
Some notes apply to several messages. These notes are referred to a "General" notes. They are referenced in the message specification "Note" column as "GEN1", "GEN2" etc. The "General" notes are listed below.
GEN1 -- Split of Cargo-IMP Messages
Cargo-IMP Messages are limited to a maximum length of 3500 characters.
A general message split procedure is used for messages which may exceed 3500 characters (except for FFM and FBL messages which have been designed with a multi-part format).
The general message split procedure is described in 4.9.2 of Chapter 1 of the

Manual.
GEN2 -- Dates and Times in Cargo-IMP Messages
Dates and times in Cargo-IMP messages are given in the "local time" at the place at which the associated action occurs.
UTC (Universal Coordinated Time) is only used in the messages relating to Embargos (FMB, FMC, FMX) where the Start of Embargo and End of Embargo are specified as Date (in UTC) and Time (in UTC).
GEN3 -- Length of Consignment Detail Line
In order to achieve a logical grouping of consignment data the "Consignment Detail" line has been defined with a theoretical maximum line length of 71 characters, i.e. the Cargo-IMP maximum line length of 69 characters has been exceeded.
It is considered extremely unlikely that all the data elements in the Consignment Detail line will be included at their maximum length. However, should this circumstance arise the one (or two) least significant characters will be "dropped" from the Nature of Goods data element to reduce the line length back to 69 characters.
GEN4 -- Airline and Company Designators
The actual format is either aa, na or an. For future planning purposes, it should be noted that the format may become "aaa".
GEN5 -- Airline and Company Designators
The two-character format (aa, na or an) may be used instead of the three-character format (aaa).
GEN6 -- Credit Card Masking
As per "Payment Card Industry Data Security Standards(PCI DSS)", masking shall be applicable to the credit card number display, a maximum of the first six and last four digits (1234 56XX XXXX 7890) can be displayed.
18. CCC/IATA CUSTOMS-AIRLINES MESSAGES
This Manual contains the Customs-Airlines Messages that were developed and agreed by the Joint Project undertaken by the Customs Cooperation Council (CCC) and IATA.
The interchange of data between Customs and Airlines is governed by a Memorandum of Understanding between the CCC and IATA, a CCC Recommendation and by IATA CSC Resolution 656.
The CCC/IATA Message Specifications contained in this Manual are amended versions to the message specifications published in Appendix `A' of the CCC/IATA

Joint Customs/Airlines Electronic Data Interchange Manual.
These CCC/IATA Message Specifications replace the Development Proposals for Airline Customs Messages that have been published in previous Editions of the Cargo-IMP Manual.
Users planning to develop or implement these Messages are requested to contact the Secretariat.

CASS ADVICE OF CORRECTION (CAC) MESSAGE

1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = CAC
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To advise the CASS Settlement Office of a correction to an air waybill transaction to be invoiced by the Settlement Office.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the computer access address of a CASS-EDI airline. To: the computer access address of the CASS-EDI mailbox.

5. MESSAGE USE
The CAC message is sent by an airline to the CASS Settlement Office to correct air waybill amounts to be invoiced to an agent. The Settlement Office will reflect the correction in the invoice produced for submitting airline.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Advice of corrections to an AWB transaction previously invoiced. Ref. CUSTOMS INVENTORY REPORT (CIR) MESSAGE

1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = CIR
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
2.1 Air Waybill Information
To declare consignment information for: · each simple air waybill (covering a single shipment); · each master air waybill (covering a group of consolidated "house" shipments); · each individual "house" waybill (where the airline is breaking down a
consolidation and will control and deliver at the house waybill level); imported or to be imported from foreign countries.
2.2 Flight Information
To supply flight information if available at time of transmission.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the access address of the Cargo Control Computer of the airline (or other party who is acting for the airline).
To: the access address of the Cargo Control Computer of Customs and/or Other Interested Parties, e.g. broker, Community System.

5. MESSAGE USE

To be sent as a result of:

Earliest:

Goods acceptance at origin.

Latest:

Goods check-in at port of arrival.

6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Advice to Customs of the expected arrival (with flight details) of a simple air waybill shipment.

9. CUSTOMS INVENTORY REPORT MESSAGE LAYOUT

CUSTOMS SUPPLEMENTARY INFORMATION (CSI) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = CSI
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
2.1 To update or amend consignment information for: · a simple air waybill (covering a single shipment); · a master air waybill (covering a group of consolidated "house" shipments); · an individual "house" waybill (where the airline is breaking down a consolidation and
will control and deliver at the house waybill level); supplied to Customs in a CIR and/or previous CSI messages. 2.2 To cancel a Customs Inventory Report.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the access address of the Cargo Control Computer of the airline (or other party who is acting for the airline).
To: the access address of the Cargo Control Computer of Customs and/or Other Interested Parties, e.g. broker, Community System.
5. MESSAGE USE
The CSI message is sent at the completion of an update/amendment input to a record in the airline in-house system.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Amend the origin of a simple air waybill shipment. Ref. Message Content 1 CSI/4 2 WBI/125-12345675 3 AMD/CH 4 WBH/DXBJFK/100/K1000
6.2 Provide Flight Information for a master air waybill not supplied in CIR Message. Ref. Message Content 1 CSI/4 2 WBI/074-72482126/M

3 AMD/AD 10 FLT/KLM010/AMSJFK/17JUL
6.3 Delete notify name and address from a house waybill broken down from a consolidation by the airline.
Ref. Message Content 1 CSI/4 2 WBI/057-12345675/M/12345678 3 AMD/DL 8 NFY/DANZAS /1230 SAINT LOUIS STREET /NEW ORLEANS/LA /US/52601
6.4 Cancel a Customs Inventory Report for a simple air waybill shipment. Ref. Message Content 1 CSI/4 2 WBI/026-47361241 3 AMD/DL 11 CCD//0

7. CSI MESSAGE SPECIFICATION

Customs Supplementary Information
Cargo-IMP Manual Edition Number/Version Number
Mail Vote 12th 01 Jan 93 13th 14th 15th 16th 17th 18th 19th 20th 21st 22nd 23rd 24th 25th 26th
3 4 4444444444 4444

9. CUSTOMS SUPPLEMENTARY INFORMATION MESSAGE LAYOUT

CUSTOMS STATUS NOTIFICATION (CSN) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = CSN
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
2.1 To update the inhouse system of the airline (or cargo terminal operator) with details of Customs determination.
2.2 Where appropriate, to update the system of the airline (or cargo terminal operator) with details of action taken by other Government agencies. 2.3 To notify the airline (or cargo terminal operator) of shipments required for examination or released for delivery.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the access address of the Cargo Control Computer of Customs and/or other Government agencies.
To: the access address of the Cargo Control Computer of the airline (or other party who is acting for the airline) and/or other Interested Parties, e.g. broker, Community System.
5. MESSAGE USE
5.1 When the Customs system initiates (or is updated with details of) action effecting the Customs status of a particular shipment a CSN message will be sent to the airline (or the cargo terminal operator).
5.2 When a Government Agency places or releases a "hold" on a particular shipment that Agency (in cooperation with Customs) will make the necessary input to trigger a "hold" or "release hold" message to the airline (or the cargo terminal operator).
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 A Customs entry for a simple air waybill has been selected for goods and document examination.
Ref. Message Content 1 CSN/3 2 WBI/125-12345675 3 FLT/BAW177/LHRJFK/17JUL 4 CAN/CA/CUSTOMS ACTIONED/002 5 DTN/17JUL/1001

6 CND/ENO900001X/25 7 CCL/9966 8 AGT/ABC
6.2 A Customs entry for a simple air waybill has been cleared. Ref. Message Content 1 CSN/3 2 WBI/085-12345675/M 3 FLT/SR222/ZRHJFK/03DEC 4 CAN/CC/CUSTOMS CLEARED/999 5 DTN/03DEC/2200 6 CND/JFK123456789/10 7 CCL/SRWAR 8 AGT/DANZAS
6.3 A Customs entry for a house waybill broken down from a consolidation by the airline is subject to physical examination.
Ref. Message Content 1 CSN/3 2 WBI/057-12345675/M/123456789 3 FLT/AF035/CDGORD/03DEC 4 CAN/PI/PHYSICAL INSPECTION/063 5 DTN/03DEC/2000 6 CND/ORD987654321/18 7 CCL/AFWAR 8 AGT/AEI

Ref. 9. Other Customs, Security and Regulatory Control Information
The composition of the OCI line depends upon which Customs, Security and Regulatory Control Information Identifier is used and must be in accordance with the OCI composition rules table below.

CSN20 Ref. Master AWB Indication (included if House Waybills are 2.4 associated with the AWB Identification).

CSN21 Ref. HWB Identification (included if carrier breaks down a 2.5 consolidation and controls and delivers at a House Waybill level).

CSN23 Ref. 7. Cargo Control Location (if required by Customs).

CSN24 Ref. 8. Nominated Agent/Consignee (if required by Customs, at least one of elements 8.3 or 8.5 must be included).

9. CUSTOMS STATUS NOTIFICATION MESSAGE LAYOUT

CASS DEBIT OR CREDIT MEMORANDUM (DCM) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = DCM
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To advise the CASS Settlement Office of an adjustment (debit or credit) to an air waybill transaction which has already been invoiced by the Settlement Office.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the computer access address of a CASS-EDI airline. To: the computer access address of the CASS-EDI mailbox.
5. MESSAGE USE
The DCM message is sent by an airline to the CASS Settlement Office to adjust air waybill amounts previously invoiced to an agent. The Settlement Office will reflect the adjustment in the subsequent invoice produced for the submitting airline.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Advice of corrections to an AWB transaction previously invoiced. Ref. Message Content 1 DCM/1 2 CIN/12345678/DC/94026 3 AGT/1234562/4321 4 CWI/114-12345675YMXCDG/K975AD/K950DL/25NOV94/FFR 5 ACS/WT10263.15CAD/WT10000.00CDL /TX1542.55CAD/TX1503.00CDL /OA20.00PAD/0A20.00PDL /CT20.00PAD/CT20.00PDL /CT11805.70CAD/CT11503.00CDL 6 NNS/102.63AD/100.00DL
7. DCM MESSAGE SPECIFICATION

9. DEBIT OR CREDIT MEMORANDUM MESSAGE LAYOUT

AIRLINE CONFIRMATION (FAC) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FAC
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
2.1 To confirm or further adjust any information previously transmitted in an Adjustment Request (FAR) message. 2.2 To further adjust any information previously transmitted in a Freight Transaction (FFT) or Air Waybill Data (FWB) message containing net billing information. 2.3 To confirm or further adjust any information previously transmitted in an Adjustment Request (FAR) message after a CASS invoice has been issued.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the computer access address of a CASS-EDI airline. To: the computer access address of the CASS-EDI mailbox; the
computer access address of a CASS-EDI agent.
5. MESSAGE USE
5.1 The Airline Confirmation message is to used by the CASS-EDI airline: 1. to confirm that any amount requested for adjustment is acceptable; 2. to further adjust any of the amounts transmitted in an Adjustment Request (FAR)
message; 3. to adjust any amount previously transmitted in a Freight Transaction (FFT) or Air
Waybill Data (FWB) message containing net billing information. For AWB Reporting, the message is only valid if a Freight Transaction (FFT) message or Air Waybill Data (FWB) message containing net billing information has previously been transmitted to the CASS-EDI mailbox. In this instance, the number of Airline Confirmation messages per air waybill number is limited to 2.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Confirmation of adjustment of the currency exchange rate. Ref. Message Content 1 FAC/1 2 AGT/1234562/4321

3 CWI/114-12345675YMX////1.235
6.2 Confirmation of adjustment of the commission amount. Ref. Message Content 1 FAC/1 2 AGT/1234562/4321 3 CWI/114-12345675YMX 7 COI//14.40

7. FAC MESSAGE SPECIFICATION

9. AIRLINE CONFIRMATION MESSAGE LAYOUT

ADVICE OF DISCREPANCY (FAD) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FAD
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To advise an apparent error in the movement of cargo and/or associated documentation found at destination, en route or at departure (see Cargo Services Conference Resolutions Manual, Resolution 610 and Recommended Practice 1600q).
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Manual/Manual-to-Manual.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the party facing the discrepancy. To: the station(s) as indicated in the Recommended Practice 1600q.
5. MESSAGE USE
--
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 "Found Cargo" discrepancy (en route or at destination). Ref. Message Content 1 FAD/1 2 083-23110006/T15K350 3 FDCA/MAD/SA271/24MAR/CDGJNB 4 OSI/RCD UNMANIFESTED WITH NO DOCS 5 REF/MADFFIB
6.2 "Found Airwaybill" discrepancy (en route or at destination). Ref. Message Content 1 FAD/1 2 083-23110006PARJNB/T15K350 3 FDAW/LIS/SA271/24MAR/CDGLIS 4 OSI/AWB IN WITH LIS DOCS
6.3 "Missing Cargo" discrepancy (at destination). Ref. Message Content

1 FAD/1 2 083-23130004BUEJNB/P12T20 3 MSCA/CPT/SA214/22MAR/EZECPT 4 OSI/MANIFESTED IN ULD LD31234SR
6.4 "Missing Air Waybill" discrepancy (at destination). Ref. Message Content 1 FAD/1 2 057-12345675LHRGVA/T2 3 MSAW/JFK/AF033/20SEP/CDGJFK 4 OSI/CARGO WITHOUT AWB 5 REF/JFKFFAF
6.5 "Off loaded" discrepancy (at departure). Ref. Message Content 1 FAD/1 2 057-12345675LHRMSP/T5 3 OFLD/CDG/AF077/19JUN/CDGJFK 4 OSI/BOTH AWB AND PACKAGES OFLD
6.6 "Short shipped" discrepancy (at departure). Ref. Message Content 1 FAD/1 2 057-12345675FRALAX/T10 3 SSPD/CDG/AF507/19JUN/CDGLAX 4 OSI/AWB ON BOARD BUT PACKAGES WILL BE FWD AF 00720JUN 5 REF/CDGFVAF
6.7 "Overcarried" discrepancy (at departure). Ref. Message Content 1 FAD/1 2 057-12345675BRUDKR/T15 3 OVCD/CDG/AF087/19JUN/CDGDKR 4 OSI/PACKAGES ON BOARD AF035-19JUN. ORD PLS FWD THEM /DIRECTLY TO DKR

5 REF/CDGFVAF
6.8 "More than one type of" discrepancy. Ref. Message Content 1 FAD/1 2 057-12345675JFKTNR/T5 3 MSCA/CDG/AF070/10JUL/JFKCDG 3 MSAW/CDG/AF070/10JUL/JFKCDG 4 OSI/PLS RUSH TO FORWARD THEM. CONSIGNEE /IS WAITING FOR URGENT DELIVERY 5 REF/CDGFVAF

9. ADVICE OF DISCREPANCY MESSAGE LAYOUT

FREIGHT AGENT INFORMATION (FAI) MESSAGE

1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FAI

2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To declare handling information for:
· each simple air waybill (covering a single shipment); · each master air waybill (covering a group of consolidated "house" shipments); · each individual "house" waybill (where the airline is breaking down a
consolidation and will control and deliver at the house waybill level); · a special cargo shipment.

3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer/Computer-to-Manual.

4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES

From:

the Airline.

To

the access address of the Cargo Control Computer of the

(Computer): Nominated Broker and/or responsible Handling Agent or

party.

To (Manual): the predestined address of the Nominated Broker and/or responsible Handling Agent or party.

5. MESSAGE USE
5.1 To inform the broker and handling parties of expected flight details before flight arrival.
5.2 To inform the handling parties on nominated broker.
5.3 To provide the broker and handling parties with information such as cargo location, arrival detail, total collect charges, storage location and customs origin code.
5.4 To provide basic air waybill information for broker and handling parties in case they cannot receive an FWB/FSB Message.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 To advise a broker/handling party of the expected arrival (with flight details) of a simple air waybill shipment with the assumption that an FWB/FSB has already been transmitted.
Ref. Message Content 1 FAI/7

2 WBI/085-12345675 3 WBH/ZRHJFK/1000/K2000 4 WBD/1000/K2000/G/NUTS
FLT/SWR101/ZRHJFK/27JAN
6.2 To advise a broker/handling party of the expected arrival (with flight details) of a master air waybill shipment with the assumption that an FWB/FSB has already been transmitted.
Ref. Message Content 1 FAI/7 2 WBI/074-12345675/M 3 WBH/AMSJFK/1/K2000 4 WBD/1/K2000/C/CONSOLIDATION 6 FLT/KLM010/AMSLHR/17JUL
6.3 To advise a broker/handling party of the expected arrival (with flight details) of a house waybill shipment with the assumption that an FWB/FSB has already been transmitted.
Ref. Message Content 1 FAI/7 2 WBI/125-12345675/23456789 3 WBH/LHRJFK/1000/K2000 4 WBD/1000/K2000/G/NUTS 6 FLT/BAA001/LHRJFK/9JUL
6.4 To advise a broker/handling party of the expected arrival (with flight details) of a simple air waybill shipment with the assumption that an FWB/FSB has not been transmitted.
Ref. Message Content 1 FAI/7 2 WBI/074-12345675 3 WBH/AMSJFK/1000/K2000 4 WBD/1000/K2000/G/NUTS 6 FLT/KLM641/AMSJFK/18JUL 7 SHP NAM/Y EXPORTER ADR/69 DE VLASCHAARD

LOC/AMSTELVEEN/NH /NL/1183KM/TE/410149 8 CNE NAM/Z IMPORTER ADR/100 BROADWAY LOC/NEW YORK/NY /US/07206 9 NFY NAM/PROVINCIAL BANK ADR/WALL STREET LOC/NEW YORK/NY /US
6.5 To advise a broker/handling party of the expected arrival (with flight details) of a simple air waybill shipment with a message comprising various information.

8. MESSAGE NOTES

FAI20 Ref. 2.4

Master AWB Indication
1. Included if information transmitted is known as related to a Master Air Waybill itself.
2. Is not included if Ref. 2.5 included.

FAI21 Ref. 2.5

HWB Identification
1. Included if carrier breaks down a consolidation and controls and delivers at a House Waybill level.
2. Is not included if Ref. 2.4 included.

FAI22 Ref. Separator (if 7.5.5 included). 7.5.4

9. FREIGHT AGENT INFORMATION MESSAGE LAYOUT

ADJUSTMENT REQUEST (FAR) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FAR
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
2.1 To request an adjustment to one of the monetary amount fields previously transmitted in a Freight Transaction (FFT) or an FWB message containing net billing information. 2.2 To request adjustments to CASS settlement amounts from a prior CASS billing period for which a CASS invoice has been issued.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the computer access address of a CASS-EDI agent. To: the computer access address of the CASS-EDI mailbox; the
computer access address of a CASS-EDI airline.
5. MESSAGE USE
5.1 This message is used by the CASS-EDI airline to consider a correction (adjustment) to information previously transmitted to the CASS-EDI mailbox in a FFT or FWB message containing net billing information.
Only one FAR message is allowed for each air waybill reported. 5.2 The message may also be to request adjustments once a CASS invoice has been issued for a specified billing period.
Only one FAR message is allowed for each air waybill reported.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Request to adjust the currency exchange rate. Ref. Message Content 1 FAR/1 2 AGT/1234562/4321 3 CWI/114-12345675YMX////1.235
6.2 Request to adjust the total weight charge, the net billing amount and the applicable taxes.
Ref. Message Content

1 FAR/1 2 AGT/1234562/4321 3 CWI/114-12345675YMX 4 ACS/WT500.00P 5 NBI/480.00CR 6 TXS//GSC33.60CR

7. FAR MESSAGE SPECIFICATION

Adjustment Request
Cargo-IMP Manual Edition Number/Version Number
12th 13th 12th 15th 16th 17th 18th 19th 20th 21st 22nd 23rd 24th 25th 26th

9. ADJUSTMENT REQUEST MESSAGE LAYOUT

FREIGHT AGENT SUPPLEMENTARY INFORMATION (FAS) MESSAGE

1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FAS

2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To update or amend handling information for:
· each simple air waybill (covering a single shipment); · each master air waybill (covering a group of consolidated "house" shipments); · each individual "house" waybill (where the airline is breaking down a
consolidation and will control and deliver at the house waybill level); · supplied to a broker or handling parties in a FAI and/or previous FAS Messages; · special cargo shipment.

3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer/Computer-to-Manual.

4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES

From:

the Airline.

To

the access address of the Cargo Control Computer of the

(Computer): Nominated Broker and/or responsible Handling Agent or

party.

To (Manual): the predestined address of the Nominated Broker and/or responsible Handling Agent, or party.

5. MESSAGE USE
To advise changes, additions and deletions to the previous FAI or FAS Messages after update/ amendment input to a record in the airline inhouse system.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 To amend the origin of a simple air waybill shipment. Ref. Message Content 1 FAS/7 2 WBI/085-12345675 3 AMD/CH 4 WBH/LINJFK/100/K1000
6.2 To add arrival documents and arrival goods details to a Master Air Waybill shipment.

Ref. Message Content 1 FAS/7 2 WBI/085-12345675/M 3 AMD/AD 14 AID/DOC/100
6.3 To delete cargo control location from a house waybill shipment. Ref. Message Content 1 FAS/7 2 WBI/085-12345675/12345678 3 AMD/DL 13 CC/PONYA
6.4 To cancel a simple air waybill shipment. Ref. Message Content 1 FAS/7 2 WBI/085-12345675 3 AMD/DL
6.5 To amend the weight of a special cargo shipment. Ref. Message Content 1 FAS/7 2 WBI/125-12345675 3 AMD/CH 4 WBH/LHRJFK/1//K38 5 WBD/1/K38/G/1 LIVE DOG

7. FAS MESSAGE SPECIFICATION

Freight Agent Supplementary Information

Cargo-IMP Manual Edition Number/Version Number

12th

18th

9. FREIGHT AGENT SUPPLEMENTARY INFORMATION MESSAGE LAYOUT

FREIGHT BOOKED LIST (FBL) MESSAGE

1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FBL

2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To supply a complete list of consignments for which space has been reserved in a particular flight, including details of the agent/airline from which the consignments will be received and, if known, inward flight details as well.

3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer/Computer-to-Manual/Manual-to-Computer/Manual-toManual.

4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES

From:

the handled carrier.

To

the access address for the Cargo Control Computer of the

(Computer): handling party (carrier or agent).

To (Manual): the destination station office of the airline operating the flight.

5. MESSAGE USE
5.1 Intention
When a carrier is controlling his own flight but the physical handling of consignments is carried out by a handling party (agent/airline), the former will issue an FBL message to the latter to provide him with a complete list of consignments for which space has been booked and any other relevant information.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Single destination list. Ref. Data Element No. 102

8. MESSAGE NOTES

FBL1

Ref. 1
Ref. 9.3.3

FBL2 Ref. 1

For a SINGLE DESTINATION flight the Freight Booked List comprises:
PART MESSAGE IDENTIFIER 1--
PART ORIGIN HEADER 2 -- Occurs once only per message.
Comprises elements 2.1 thru 2.7.
PART DESTINATION HEADER 3 -- Occurs once only per message.
Comprises elements 3.1 thru 3.3.
PART BOOKED CARGO 4 -- Repeated for each booked consignment.
Comprises complete sets of consignment information (elements 4.1 thru 9.3) for each booked consignment.
PART LIST TRAILER 5 -- Occurs once only per message.
Comprises an end code identifying the:
· end of a message (notification that a further message relating to the same flight manifest will follow, see note FBL4);
· end of list (notification that a list transmission is complete).
For a MULTI-DESTINATION flight the Freight Booked List comprises:
PART MESSAGE IDENTIFIER 1--
PART MANIFEST HEADER 2 -- Occurs once only per
message.

PART 3-- PART 4--
PART 5--

DESTINATION HEADER
BOOKED CARGO Repeated for each consignment.
LIST TRAILER Occurs once only per message.

Repeated for each destination airport

FBL3 Ref. 3.2
FBL4 Ref. 2.1

If no consignment is booked for a particular destination airport the DESTINATION HEADER for that airport is extended to include element 3.2 (NIL Cargo Indication). The BOOKED CARGO details which would normally follow a DESTINATION HEADER are omitted.

Ref. 11 A Freight Booked List can be transmitted as a sequence of FBL messages. (This is sometimes necessary due to message length constraints imposed by the transmission network.)
Each individual message must commence with MESSAGE IDENTIFIER, MANIFEST HEADER and DESTINATION HEADER. Within the ORIGIN HEADER, a message sequence number identifies the sequence in which individual FBL messages have been transmitted.
The use of the code "CONT" (continued) in the LIST TRAILER at the end of a FBL message signifies that a further message relating to the same flight manifest will be transmitted.
The use of the code "LAST" in the LIST TRAILER at the end of a FBL message signifies that the list transmission is complete.

FBL20 Ref. 3.2
FBL21 Ref. 4.5
FBL22 Ref. 4.6.2

NIL Cargo Indication (if no cargo is booked for this airport). Density Group (if 4.4 not included). Number of Pieces (zero if unknown).

9. FREIGHT BOOKED LIST MESSAGE LAYOUT

FREIGHT BOOKED LIST REQUEST (FBR) MESSAGE

1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FBR

2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To request a Freight Booked List (FBL) message.

3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer/Computer-to-Manual/Manual-to-Computer/Manual-toManual.

4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES

From:

the handling party (carrier or agent).

To

the access address of the Cargo Control Computer of the

(Computer): handled carrier.

To (Manual): the destination station office of the handled carrier.

5. MESSAGE USE
By handling parties (carriers or agents) to request a Freight Booked List (FBL) message when needed.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Request for a single destination list. Ref. Message Content 1 FBR/2 2 FLT/KL791/19MAR/ZRHLIS 3 REF/ZRHFZSR
6.2 Request for a multiple destination list. Ref. Message Content 1 FBR/2 2 FLT/KL775/09FEB/ZRH 3 REF/ZRHFZSR

9. FREIGHT BOOKED LIST REQUEST MESSAGE LAYOUT

COURIER BAGGAGE VOUCHER (FBV) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FBV
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To exchange courier baggage voucher details in accordance with Recommended Practice 1600g.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the access address of the Cargo Control Computer of a participant involved in the movement of courier baggage.
To: the access address of the Cargo Control Computer of a participant involved in the movement of courier baggage.
5. MESSAGE USE
To eliminate the need to re-enter Courier Baggage Voucher data which has already been entered by a participant in the movement of courier baggage.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Exchange for courier bags going from Los Angeles to Tokyo. Ref. Message Content 1 FBV/1 2 CBV/131-ABCD12345 3 AIR/JAPAN AIRLINES /4-11 HIGASHI-SHINAGAWA 2-CHOME /TOKYO /JP 4 CBS/FEDEX /1980 NONCONNAH BLVD /MEMPHIS/TENNESSEE /US/38132 5 CBR/FEDEX /NARITA AIRPORT /TOKYO

/JP 7 RTG/LAXTYO 12 BGD/3K30 17 CER/H.AMLING 18 ISU/01MAY00/LOS ANGELES/I.KANASE

CHARGES CORRECTION ACKNOWLEDGEMENT (FCA) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FCA
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To notify the carrier issuing a Charges Correction Request (FCC) Message that the request has been received and actioned.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Manual-to-Manual.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the party receiving the Charges Correction Request (FCC) Message.
To: the party (as indicated in the signature of the FCC Message) from which the Charges Correction Request (FCC) Message was received.
5. MESSAGE USE
--

9. CHARGES CORRECTION ACKNOWLEDGEMENT MESSAGE LAYOUT

FREIGHT CASS BILLING (FCB) MESSAGE

1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FCB

2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To advise the CASS Settlement Office of specific billing data of an accountable transaction made by a cargo agent on behalf of an appointing airline participating in CASS.

3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.

4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES

From (Computer):
To (Computer):

the access address of the computer of either a cargo agent or an airline.
the access address of the computer of the CASS Settlement Office.

5. MESSAGE USE
This message is sent as a result of an Air Waybill Issuance, and in addition to a FWB Message, to the CASS Settlement Office.

6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Freight CASS Billing. Ref. Message Content 1 FCB/2 2 WBI/057-12345675 3 CBI/Z99/139/K520

7. FCB MESSAGE SPECIFICATION

9. CHARGES CORRECTION REQUEST MESSAGE LAYOUT

CASS INVOICE (FCI) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FCI
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To advise the CASS-EDI agent of each air waybill transaction and the amount payable in a given CASS billing period.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the computer access address of the CASS Settlement Office. To: the computer access address of the CASS-EDI mailbox;
or the computer access address of the CASS-EDI agent or airline mailbox; or the computer access address of the CASS-EDI airline or the CASSEDI agent.
5. MESSAGE USE
The CASS EDI Invoice results from the processing of either the Freight Transaction (FFT) or the net Air Waybill Data (FWB) message, with the Adjustment Request (FAR) and the Airline Confirmation (FAC) messages transmitted by CASS-EDI airlines and the CASS-EDI agents to the CASS-EDI mailbox. The CASS Invoice message is transmitted to the CASS-EDI mailbox and/or directly to the CASS-EDI airlines and agents.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Invoice to CASS-EDI agent for more than one airline for net invoice amounts only. Ref. Message Content 1 FCI/2 2 CIH/13OCT93/93020/15NOV93 4 AGT/1234562/4321 5 AIR/014 6 ABI/26488125//NI480.00 6 ABI/23511716//NI6000.00

10 ABT/CN6480.00 5 AIR/018 6 ABI/12345675//NI1000.00 6 ABI/23456764//NI1855.00 10 ABT/CN2855.00 18 GTI/CN93335.00
6.2 Invoice to CASS-EDI airline: 6.2.1 Message transmitted to CASS-EDI mailbox for collection/distribution.
Ref. Message Content 1 FCI/2 2 CIH/13OCT93/93020/15NOV93 3 AIR/450 4 AGT/1234562/4321 /GS100321654/QS1002302 6 ABI/12312322/P/NI65.61/WT65.61 7 ABS/YYZNYC/K59.65/20OCT93/CAD/ACME123/L/1.10 6 ABI/12332125/P/NI1966.50/WT2120.00/SI50.00 /CO103.50 7 ABS/YYZSYD/K662.50/05NOV93/CAD/ACME567//3.20 6 ABI/12345678/C/NI212.50CR/WT1750.00/OA125.00 /CO87.50 7 ABS/YYZTYO/K269.23/11NOV93/CAD/ACME987//6.50 8 PPD/WT2185.00 9 COL/WT1750.00/0A125.00 10 ABT/SI50.00/CO191.00/CN1919.61 11 AWT/K991.38/3.97 18 GTI/CN1944.61

9. CASS INVOICE MESSAGE LAYOUT

CASS REMITTANCE (FCR) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FCR
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To advise the CASS-EDI airline and the CASS-EDI agent of the total amounts due by each party.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the computer access address of the CASS Settlement Office. To: the computer access address of the CASS-EDI mailbox;
the computer access address of a CASS-EDI airline; the computer access address of a CASS-EDI agent.
5. MESSAGE USE
5.1 The CASS Remittance message summarises the total amounts due by each participant in the CASS-EDI settlement process after the CASS invoice procedure has been completed.
The message is also transmitted to the CASS mailbox to advise the CASS-EDI agents and airlines of the totals amounts due. 5.2 The CASS Remittance message may also be used for local CASS message requirements and to transmit information to the CASS Settlement Bank.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Remittance notice for one agent and more than one airline. Ref. Message Content 1 FCR/1 2 CBP/93024 3 CBD/1234562/4321/014/855.00 3 CBD/1234562/4321/057/1000.00
7. FCR MESSAGE SPECIFICATION
CASS Remittance Cargo-IMP Manual Edition Number/Version Number

12th 13th 14th 15th 16th 17th 18th 19th 20th 21st 22nd 23rd 24th 25th 26th
- - 1111111111111

9. CASS REMITTANCE MESSAGE LAYOUT

CASS VOID/CANCEL AIR WAYBILL (FCV) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FCV
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To cancel or void an air waybill number and any transactions applied to that air waybill in the CASS EDI procedures.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the computer access address of the CASS-EDI airline. To: the computer access address of the CASS-EDI mailbox; the
computer access address of the CASS-EDI agent.
5. MESSAGE USE
A CASS-EDI airline may need to void or cancel an air waybill and to cancel any CASS transactions that may have been applied against that air waybill. Once this message is transmitted to the CASS mailbox, any transactions in the mailbox are also voided. The CASS Settlement Office will process voided air waybills as part of the settlement process. Cancelled air waybills will not be processed.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Voiding of one air waybill and cancellation of another. Ref. Message Content 1 FCV/1 2 VCD/1234562/4321//014-26488125/V 2 VCD/1234562/4321//125-27469212/C
7. FCV MESSAGE SPECIFICATION
CASS Void/Cancel Air Waybill Cargo-IMP Manual Edition Number/Version Number
12th 13th 14th 15th 16th 17th 18th 19th 20th 21st 22nd 23rd 24th 25th 26th
- - 1111111111111

9. CASS VOID/CANCEL AIR WAYBILL MESSAGE LAYOUT

DISCREPANCY ANSWER (FDA) MESSAGE
1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FDA
2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
To reply to an Advice of Discrepancy (FAD) Message to advise of action that will be taken or should be taken.
3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Manual-to-Manual.
4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES
From: the party to which the Advice of Discrepancy (FAD) Message was addressed.
To: either: the Advising Office Message Address optionally indicated as Ref. 5.3 in the Advice of Discrepancy (FAD) Message; or: if not included in the FAD Message -- the party (as indicated in the signature of the FAD message) from which the Advice of Discrepancy (FAD) Message was received.
5. MESSAGE USE
5.1 Situations Where an FDA Message is Not Required
These are described in Recommended Practice 1600q.
6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Reply to a "Found Cargo" discrepancy. Ref. Message Content 1 FDA/1 2 083-23110006PARJNB/T15K350 3 FDCA/LIS/SA271/24MAR/CDGJNB 4 OSI/CONSIGNMENT DEST FOR JNB CNE SMITH IMPORTS JNB /DOCS FOLLOW SA261 25MAR LISJNB 5 REF/NBOFFSA
6.2 Reply to a "Found Air Waybill" discrepancy. Ref. Message Content

1 FDA/1 2 083-23110006PARJNB/T15K350 3 FDAW/LIS/SA271/24MAR/CDGLIS 4 OSI/FWD AWB TO JNB ASAP.PLS ADV FLT USED
6.3 Reply to a "Missing Cargo" discrepancy. Ref. Message Content 1 FDA/1 2 083-23130004BUEJNB/P12T20 3 MSCA/CDG/SA214/22MAR/EZECPT 4 OSI/WILL FWD SA214 24MAR 5 REF/CPTFTSA
6.4 Reply to a "Missing Air Waybill" discrepancy. Ref. Message Content 1 FDA/1 2 057-12345675LHRGVA/T2 3 MSAW/JFK/AF033/20SEP/CDGJFK 4 OSI/WILL FWD AF033 21SEP
6.5 Reply to an "Off-loaded Cargo" discrepancy. Ref. Message Content 1 FDA/1 2 057-12345675LHRMSP/T5 3 OFLD/CDG/AF077/19JUN/CDGJFK 4 OSI/NO PROBLEM ON ARRIVAL
6.6 Reply to a "Short Shipped Cargo" discrepancy. Ref. Message Content 1 FDA/1 2 057-12345675FRALAX/T10 3 SSPD/CDG/AF007/14JUN/CDGLAX 4 OSI/CUSTOMS ADVISED
6.7 Reply to an "Over Carried Cargo" discrepancy. Ref. Message Content

1 FDA/1 2 057-12345675BRUDKR/T15 3 OVCD/ORD/AF087/19JUN/CDGRIO 4 OSI/NO MORE PROBLEMS AS ORD FWD PACKAGES

8. MESSAGE NOTES

FDD1 Ref. 1 The numbers shown in column DGD Box No. corresponds with the circled box numbers of the DGD form included as ATTACHMENT 1.

FDD2 Ref. 1.1

Due to Cargo-IMP character set restrictions, the comma cannot be transmitted. Therefore, two full stops will be used to indicate a comma within the proper shipping name, technical name or radioactive name.

FDD3 Ref. 2.3

Ref. 21 A Declaration for Dangerous Goods Data can be transmitted as a sequence of FDD messages. (This is sometimes necessary due to message length constraints imposed by the transmission network).
Each individual FDD message must commence with MESSAGE IDENTIFIER and DGD HEADER. Within the DGD HEADER a message sequence number identifies the sequence in which individual messages have been transmitted.
The use of the code "CONT" (continued) in the DGD TRAILER at the end of a FDD message signifies that a further message

relating to the same Declaration for Dangerous Goods will be transmitted. The use of the code "LAST" in the DGD TRAILER at the end of a FDD message signifies that the Declaration for Dangerous Goods transmission is complete.
FDD20 Ref. Separator (if 3.5.5 included). 3.5.4
FDD21 Ref. Coded Location 3.6
Ref. Contact Details (total characters of 3.6 through 3.7 cannot exceed 3.7 69).
FDD22 Ref. Separator (if 3.6.4 and/or 3.7 included). 3.6.3
FDD23 Ref. Separator (if 4.5.5 included). 4.5.4
FDD24 Ref. Coded Location 4.6

9. DECLARATION FOR DANGEROUS GOODS DATA MESSAGE LAYOUT

ATTACHMENT 1

ATTACHMENT 1
EXAMPLE 1

ATTACHMENT 1
EXAMPLE 2

ATTACHMENT 1
EXAMPLE 3

ATTACHMENT 1
EXAMPLE 4

AWB SPACE ALLOCATION ANSWER (FFA) MESSAGE

1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FFA

2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
2.1 To reply to an AWB Space Allocation Request (FFR) Message indicating that requested space can or cannot be confirmed.
2.2 To reply to an AWB Space Allocation Request (FFR) Message indicating that request is being processed and that further FFA message will follow.
2.3 To reply to an AWB Space Allocation Request (FFR) Message confirming or refusing the accepted updates by showing the new space allocation details.

3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer/Manual-to-Computer/Computer-to-Manual/Manual-toManual.

4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES

From (Manual): From (Computer): To (Manual):
To (Computer):

the airline controlling the requested flight(s).
the access address of the cargo control computer of the airline controlling the requested flight(s).
the party (as indicated in the booking reference line of the FFR message) who requested space or modifications on the flight(s).
the access address of the cargo control computer (as indicated in the booking reference line of the FFR message) that requested space or modifications on the flight(s).

5. MESSAGE USE
5.1 Multi-Airline Multi-Flight FFA Message
An airline will reply to a multi-airline multi-flight AWB Space Allocation Request (FFR) Message using an FFA message which relates only to those space allocations on flight(s) controlled by that airline.
5.2 Situations Where FFA Messages May Not Be Required
When cancellations (space allocation code "XX"), sale reports (space allocation codes "CA" and "SS") or background information (space allocation codes "HK" and "HL") are included in an AWB Space Allocation Request (FFR) Message and accepted as such, an FFA Message may be sent at the discretion of the message recipient.

6. MESSAGE EXAMPLES
6.1 Confirmation of AWB Space Allocation (code KK). Ref. Message Content 1 FFA/4 2 125-1234565FRAPHL/T12K950/BOOKS /VAL 3 BA171/19MAR/LHRJFK/KK 6 REF/FRAFCBA
6.2 Rejection of AWB Space Allocation (code UU). Ref. Message Content 1 FFA/4 2 057-12345675BHXJFK/T5K400 3 AF077/19MAY/CDGJFK/UU 6 REF/BHXFRBA/1234
6.3 Rejection of AWB Space Allocation because the flight does not operate (code UN). Ref. Message Content 1 FFA/4 2 125-12345675LHRLAX/T12K900 3 SR3309/11JUL/ZRHLAX/UN 6 REF/LHRFWBA
6.4 Wait Listing of AWB Space Allocation (code LL). Ref. Message Content 1 FFA/4 2 085-12345675ZRHLAX/T10K250 3 AA123/10MAY/JFKLAX/LL 6 REF/ZRHFRSR
6.5 Holding Confirmation of AWB Space Allocation (code HK). Ref. Message Content 1 FFA/4 2 125-21210000EDIYMX/T7K95 3 BA073/16MAR/LHRYMX/HK

6 REF/GLAFRBA
6.6 Holding Request of AWB Space Allocation (code HN). Ref. Message Content 1 FFA/4 2 125-21220006GLAYMX/T75K1550 3 BA073/16MAR/LHRYMX/HN 6 REF/GLAFRBA
6.7 Holding Wait Listing of AWB Space Allocation (code HL). Ref. Message Content 1 FFA/4 2 057-12345675JEDMIA/T3K200 3 EA2222/10JUL/IAHMIA/HL 6 REF/PARFRAF
6.8 Answer on more than one flight. Ref. Message Content 1 FFA/4 2 160-76543213HKGYSB/T4K160 3 AC857/19MAR/LHRYYZ/KK 3 AC363/20MAR/YYZYSB/UU 6 REF/HKGFQCX
6.9 Answer with Special Service Request (SSR) and Other Service Information (OSI) on more than one line along with Shipment Reference Information.
Ref. Message Content 1 FFA/4 2 021-77777770MSPLHR/P5K5750T9 3 AA001/19MAR/JFKLHR/KK 4 SSR/TEMP RESTRICTION OK /SPL CARE OK 5 OSI/NEED TO RCV CGO BY 1800 19MAR /AND TO MEET THE AGENT 6 REF/MSPFCNW/4923ACA 7 SRI/ABCD-12345

6.10 Acceptance of a time definite service request using the Shipment Reference Information (SRI) line.

9. AWB SPACE ALLOCATION ANSWER MESSAGE LAYOUT

AIRLINE FLIGHT MANIFEST (FFM) MESSAGE

1. STANDARD MESSAGE IDENTIFIER = FFM

2. MESSAGE FUNCTION
2.1 To notify details of consignments loaded onto a specified flight.
2.2 (optional)To notify details of the onward movements of consignment loaded onto a specified flight to enable transfers to be preplanned.

3. MESSAGE APPLICATION
Computer-to-Computer/Computer-to-Manual/Manual-to-Computer/Manual-toManual.

4. MESSAGE EXCHANGE PARTIES

From:

the party generating the manifest.

To

the access address for the cargo control computer of the

(Computer): airline, shed operator or handling agent receiving the

consignment.

To (Manual): the destination station office of the airline receiving the consignment.

5. MESSAGE USE
5.1 Multi-Airport FFM
A separate FFM is normally generated for each airport of unloading and only the appropriate message is sent to each unloading point.
However, when generating an FFM to be sent to a central cargo system (which can control the distribution of the load information to each unloading point) a single FFM for multi-airports of unloading may be. sent. Cargo Control systems must therefore be able to receive all of the following options:
· a series of single destination FFM messages (see note FFM1); · a single multi destination FFM message (see note FFM2); · a sequence of part FFM messages (see note FFM4).

8. MESSAGE NOTES

FFM1

Ref. 1

For a SINGLE DESTINATION flight the Airline Flight Manifest comprises:
PART 1 MESSAGE IDENTIFIER --

PART 2 MANIFEST HEADER

--

Occurs once only per message.

Comprises elements 2.1 thru 2.7.

PART 3 DESTINATION HEADER

--

Occurs once only per message.

Comprises elements 3.1 thru 3.3.

PART 4 BULK LOADED CARGO

--

Repeated for each bulk loaded

PART 5 --
PART 6 --

consignment. Comprises complete sets of consignment information (elements 4.1 thru 9.10).
ULD LOADED CARGO Repeated for each ULD. Comprises combination sets of information describing each ULD (elements 10.1 thru 11.4) and each of the consignments in the ULD (elements 12.1 thru 17.10 -- repeated).
MANIFEST TRAILER Occurs once only per message. Comprises an end code identifying the:

· end of a message (notification that a further message relating to the same flight manifest will follow, see note FFM4);
· end of manifest (notification that a manifest transmission is complete).

FFM2

Ref. 1. For a MULTI-DESTINATION flight a separate airline flight manifest message is normally sent for each destination airport. However, where required a full manifest message for all consignments loaded at one point of loading may also be sent comprising:
PART 1 MESSAGE IDENTIFIER --

PART 2 MANIFEST HEADER

--

Occurs once only per message.

PART 3 DESTINATION

--

HEADER

PART 4 --
PART 5 --

BULK

LOADED

CARGO

Repeated for each

bulk

loaded

consignment.

ULD CARGO
Repeated ULD.

LOADED for each

Repeated for each destination airport

PART 6 MANIFEST TRAILER

--

Occurs once only per

message.

FFM3 Ref. 3.3

If no consignment is loaded for a particular destination airport the DESTINATION HEADER for that airport is extended to include element 3.2 (NIL Cargo Indication). The BULK LOADED and ULD LOADED details which would normally follow a DESTINATION HEADER are omitted.

FFM4

Ref. 2.1
Ref. 18 An airline Flight Manifest can be transmitted as a sequence of FFM messages. (This is sometimes necessary due to message length constraints imposed by the transmission network).
Each individual FFM message must commence with MESSAGE IDENTIFIER, MANIFEST HEADER and DESTINATION HEADER. In addition a ULD Detail Line must be included before any sets of consignment information relating to ULD Loaded Cargo (even if this ULD information has also been included in previous FFM messages relating to the same flight). Within the MANIFEST HEADER message sequence number identifies the sequence in which individual messages have been transmitted.
The complete Consignment Detail/Consignment Onward Movement Information (elements 4.1 thru 9.10) relating to one consignment must always be sent in one FFM message.
The use of the code "CONT" (continued) in the MANIFEST TRAILER at the end of a FFM message signifies that a further message relating to the same flight manifest will be transmitted.
The use of the code "LAST" in the MANIFEST TRAILER at the end of a FFM message signifies that the manifest transmission is complete.

FFM6 Ref. A full description of the Shipment Description Code T, P, S and D 4.3.2 is given in Code List, 1.15.

FFM7

Ref. 9. Other Customs, Security and Regulatory Control Information
